title_en: Taharah: Al-Dasuqi's Refinements on Maliki Purification Law
title_ar: الطهارة: تدقيقات الدسوقي في مسائل الطهارة
content_en:
Al-Dasuqi's hashiyah on the taharah (purification) sections of Al-Sharh al-Kabir represents some of his most technically precise and analytically independent scholarship. Working through ad-Dardiri's explanations of Mukhtasar Khalil, al-Dasuqi identifies places where additional precision is needed, notes disagreements among Maliki authorities that ad-Dardiri glossed over, and occasionally takes positions that differ from his teacher's readings.

On the question of water and its legal categories, al-Dasuqi refines the discussion of what constitutes a 'change' in water quality that renders it impure. He distinguishes between changes in color, taste, and smell caused by physical filth (which render the water impure) and changes caused by prolonged standing, mixing with earth, or natural processes (which do not affect the water's purifying capacity). This distinction — standard across the schools but requiring careful elaboration — is laid out systematically in his hashiyah with attention to the textual sources and the positions of earlier Maliki scholars such as Ibn Rushd al-Jadd and al-Qarafi.

The requirement of dalk (rubbing) in ablution is a signature Maliki position, and al-Dasuqi's hashiyah provides an extended discussion of its basis and application. He clarifies what counts as sufficient rubbing, whether rubbing through thin clothing satisfies the requirement, and how the school handles situations in which a person's physical condition makes rubbing difficult. His analysis draws on Ibn ShAS, al-'Adawi, and other Maliki authorities to provide a nuanced picture of how the school's scholars understood this distinctive requirement.

Al-Dasuqi's treatment of the conditions for tayammum (dry ablution) is particularly detailed. The Maliki school permits tayammum not only when water is completely unavailable but also when its use would likely cause harm — for illness, extreme cold, or wounds. Al-Dasuqi clarifies the threshold of harm that triggers the permission for tayammum, the surfaces that may be used (clean earth or anything with 'the quality of earth'), and the precise manner of performance required. He notes disagreements within the school about whether tayammum can be performed before the time of prayer enters or must await it.

On ritual impurity (najasah), al-Dasuqi's hashiyah engages carefully with the Maliki school's approach, which differs from the Shafi'i school in several respects. The Maliki school does not require sevenfold washing for dog-related impurity — the standard washing until traces are removed applies. Al-Dasuqi traces the evidence for this Maliki position in detail, engaging with the prophetic hadith about washing containers touched by dogs and the Maliki interpretive methodology that yields a different conclusion from the Shafi'i reading of the same texts.

The hashiyah also addresses wine (khamr) and its purification. The Maliki school holds that wine is impure (najis), and contaminated items must be washed to remove it. Al-Dasuqi engages with the question of vinegar produced from wine — which is pure according to all schools — and discusses the transition point at which wine becomes vinegar and loses its impurity status.

Al-Dasuqi's treatment of menstrual impurity (hayd) and postpartum bleeding (nifas) extends ad-Dardiri's explanations with additional discussion of the minimum and maximum durations recognized by the Maliki school, the rules governing women whose bleeding is irregular or prolonged (mustahadah), and the conditions under which the husband may approach his wife after her period has ended but before she has performed ghusl.

Throughout these discussions, al-Dasuqi's hashiyah demonstrates what distinguishes a great supercommentary from a mediocre one: genuine engagement with the legal issues, independent analysis grounded in the school's methodology, and a commitment to clarity that serves both students and practitioners.
